author: @strongforce8466
text: Does vinyls bend aswell ? Aha I guess it's a harder carbon due to the looks of it but might work, no idea, brilliant videos as always, this is an awesome way to recycle old cd's (scratches..) and pc fans.. wonder how much bigger fan blades would generate or also an array of these, eventually paint it with waterproof paint to make it last longer too, not sure how polycarbonate resist to water

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
Water, just fine. Ultraviolet light, maybe not so much.
And a quick Google Search showed me wrong.
PVC has trouble in the sun, without stabilizers.
Polycarbonates are fine, and actually used in some Solar Panels.

author: @Dr-Sy
text: What about putting the fan inside a drain pipe along with a funnel so that when it rains it comes off the roof into the gutter and then into the downpipe, in the top of the downpipe you place a small funnel to turn the flow of water into a stronger flow of water which then would push the fan blades around. Just a suggestion as it rains a lot here.

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text: If you're on a hill it might be better to just channel all the downspouts into a Holding Pond, and, once it's full, use the hill as a head for a more efficient turbine, based on the Head Height.

replies.author: @Dr-Sy
replies.text: @TimeSurfer206 I thought of over hanging some type of turbine off the edge of the cliff, the updraft from the winds hitting the mountain produce huge amounts of current.

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text: @Dr-Sy Check out the Ugrinsky Turbine, I think that would be great mounted horizontally there.

author: @markdebono1273
text: Hi Rob, I have successfully built a few generators but the question that constantly goes round in my mind is how to put them to good practical use, e.g. to charge a mobile phone. I would be really greatful if you could cover this topic. Thanks a lot!

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
For that, you will need a connector and a Voltage Limiter, to keep the output voltage at the correct level.
Personally, I would suggest an MPPT Charge Controller that has USB Charging Ports.
You'll want a battery to go with, but, both of those can be had on the small side relatively cheap, and are just _so awesome_for future projects.

replies.author: @markdebono1273
replies.text: @TimeSurfer206 Thanks for your input. It is greatly appreciated!

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
@markdebono1273 You're welcome! I do like "Off the shelf products" when they're cheap.
Especially if they'll ever be hooked up to my house, as my Insurance Company seems to like UL rated, or equivalent.
And by hooked up, I mean permanently plugged in.
Mind, anything we do before the Charge Controller is pretty much up for grabs, as long as we're following some kind of Electrical Code.
As an Electrician, I tend to be retentive about over-fusing things.

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
For those that don't want to use the saw, you can cut a warm CD with a scissors.  Getting the temp just right is the trick. Too warm and the CD warps, too cool and it shatters, Which would be heart breaking near the end on this project

I've used a toaster oven that was cooling down after a toast cycle, but a heat guns should work too. Warning, the CD will be hot to the touch but does start to cool down...and then it's prone to shattering to heat may need to be reapply ed a few times

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text: I am picturing hanging an infrared Heat Lamp at the "Sweet spot" over the bench to keep a few of these at the proper temp. Work one for a cut or two, drop it back under the lamp, grab another. Rinse, repeat.

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text: @TimeSurfer206 I got a cheap toaster oven put it on light tost setting, then when the toaster "dinged" indicating toast was done, that's when I opened the door and put a couple in for about 30 seconds :)

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text: @colleenforrest7936 That is also an excellent idea! Thank you.

replies.author: @colleenforrest7936
replies.text: @TimeSurfer206 Play with it a little and you'll get a feel for the heat and timing for your setup.  Be warned though if you are taping pieces together.  The silver backing can crumble off fairly easily.

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text: @colleenforrest7936 Good, that means removing it will be easy. I have ideas beyond just turbine blades.

author: @ile84
text: I thought you'd glue that CD on the other side of the PC fan so it catches all the wind, like that "Rose turbine" you made not too long ago.

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text: Given that a computer fan runs at 12 volts, at maybe half an amp, to spin at 100s of revs per minute, doesn't that imply that to extract a similar output power, it is necessary to connect a gearbox between the fan blades and the generator, to increase the rotation speed to similar to that achieved when it was used as a motor?

replies.author: @TimeSurfer206
replies.text:
Yes, but that adds significant cost. These are virtually "A dime a dozen," so I don't mind rectifying them all and stacking their outputs in series.
If we were talking a turbine a bit larger, it would be worth, especially if one used a surface area increaser like the Darwin Turbine.

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text: May I suggest to take a bicycle wheel and some wide tape about 12-15 cm wide. The spokes are arranged that on the outer rim they all are on the circumference in one surface. The inner nacelle, where the ball bearings are, the spokes are arranged in a twisted position. (L/R) Pick the right spokes, tape them in and you have yourself a windturbine

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome cheers mate - I like that idea - very nice and very easy to replicate

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Apparently a little known fact.  The American multi blade wind turbin though perfect for powering a  pump that pumps water, it has about the worst COP,  below Savonius  even.  

Some may disagree. But before anyone gets in big long discussionns, the point in contention starts with a popularized but incorrect chart that wrongly  swapped the American Multiblade and the Savonius. But as proof of the Savinous performance, one can easilly find many university studies with a Savonius peak cop well above those predicted by the chart that places the American multiblade over the Savonius. Sorry but your charts are wrong if they place the America multiblade over the Savonius.

As to this project, it works like a hawt and savonius  is really for vawt, and CDs are ubiquitous to source.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: is that right? very interesting mate - cheers

author: @paulbrouyere1735
text: I don’t like to oppose you, but here I believe you’re missing the point. The exterior should be flat and the interior should have twisted blades. This is due to the relative wind each part of the blade is ‘seeing’ relative to the radius

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: on turbines generally you are quite right mate - this is because the speed increases as you go along the length of the blade - but this is a few centimetres long - so the speed difference is going to be minimal really - so yes - something to worry about in a larger turbine but not really much of a worry here

It's at this point I get lost in the research I've done as most forums say your wasting your time go with solar but this is purely out of interest I'm not trying to power my home.

author: @garybooting_co_uk5866
text: I am constantly frustrated by your videos, you have some great concepts but I do not see storage of the generated electricity. Please can we move on to actually harnessing!!!!!

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text: Word! Creating power is nothing but tinkering if the power's not usable. Rob should have plenty of selfmade capacitors and now also phone batteries. I wonder why he keeps so many of us frustrated.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Oddly enough I get very frustrated that sometimes folks can't see a thing through for themselves. Can you really not see all you have to do is stick a controller on it and then bolt that to some batteries? I mean that bit is easy and tons of people have done it to death

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mostly mate because I don't see the need. The standard route is controller, battery, inverter, home - they are pretty much just plug in components off the shelf - but I am pretty sure you know that - all you do is stick a controller on it and bolt that to a battery - how difficult can that be?

replies.author: @garybooting_co_uk5866
replies.text: I guess what I was really looking for is a homemade storage system that is "Awesome" 😜

replies.author: @Teknopottu
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Love your channel, it's the best youtube has to offer. You are an inspirational guide for thousands. There are people on many levels of knowledge and courage here. Perhaps if you showed us, say, how to connect one of the turbines into a controller onto a battery onto an inverter it would not seem too hard not to try it yourself. A DIY-generator producing something between couple of volts to hundreds of volts is not just plug&playing it into any controller, people need guidance to feel safer. If too time consuming/off the subject, maybe give people subjects to take notice. Show them the path, not just the general direction of a forest. I've seen a dozen videos about PC fan or hand crank generator lighting an LED while it could charge a powerbank, do something usefull. Something people would need and would be proud they made.

If not, I'm still enjoying your stuff.
